file_name: Mallanaikar et al. - 2025 - Multimodal learning for skin lesion segmentation and closed visual question answering.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning models for skin lesion segmentation and closed visual question answering. Specifically, it employs a Multi-Scale Feature Fusion Network (MSFNet) with boundary and reverse attention modules for segmentation on 314 dermatology images, and a multimodal architecture combining Vision Transformer (ViT), Local Binary Patterns (LBP), and CLIP for VQA on the DermaVQA-DAS dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and evaluation of a unified deep learning framework for skin lesion segmentation and closed visual question answering. It details specific methodologies (MSFNet, multimodal VQA architecture), datasets used (MEDIQA-MAGIC 2025, DermaVQA-DAS), training strategies, and reports new quantitative results (Dice coefficients, accuracy scores). Although published in the CLEF 2025 Working Notes, it describes a specific technical study with original results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Multimodal Learning for Skin Lesion Segmentation and Closed Visual Question Answering
brief_summary: This study presents a unified deep learning framework for skin lesion segmentation and closed-ended visual question answering (VQA) to support dermatological diagnostics. For segmentation, the authors employed a Multi-Scale Feature Fusion Network (MSFNet) with boundary and reverse attention modules, trained on 314 images from the MEDIQA-MAGIC 2025 dataset. The model achieved a mean Dice coefficient of 0.7021 and a mean Jaccard index of 0.5410, demonstrating effective lesion boundary delineation. In parallel, a multimodal VQA system combining Vision Transformer, Local Binary Patterns, and CLIP was evaluated on 56 image-question pairs from the DermaVQA-DAS dataset. This VQA model attained an overall accuracy of 56.98%, with higher performance in specific question categories like CQID012 and CQID035. The results highlight the potential of integrating segmentation and VQA pipelines for clinical decision support and automated medical interpretation.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study focuses on binary segmentation of skin lesions in general using the MEDIQA-MAGIC 2025 DermaVQA-DAS dataset. While the abstract mentions supporting 'early melanoma detection', the experiments do not distinguish specific histological classes (e.g., melanoma vs. nevus) but treat the target as a binary lesion mask.

Mentioned in the abstract as a clinical context for which the segmentation model supports early detection ('supporting its effectiveness in aiding early melanoma detection'), but not explicitly studied as a distinct class in the segmentation or VQA experiments.

all_task_types.details: The paper performs binary segmentation of skin lesions using MSFNet on 314 dermatology images. The model outputs a binary probability map via sigmoid activation, evaluated using Dice coefficient and Jaccard index.

The paper performs closed-ended Visual Question Answering (VQA) as a multi-class classification problem. The model classifies inputs into one of 12 answer categories using a softmax classifier, evaluated using macro-averaged accuracy.

datasets_used.name: MEDIQA-MAGIC 2025 / DermaVQA-DAS
datasets_used.description: A dataset comprising pixel-annotated dermatology images used for the MEDIQA-MAGIC 2025 segmentation task and the DermaVQA-DAS (Dermatology Assessment Schema) dataset for closed-ended visual question answering. It contains patient-generated dermatology images with gold-standard masks and structured questions.

use.training: Used for training the MSFNet segmentation model with gold-standard masks provided in the dataset.
use.validation: Used for validation loss monitoring to trigger early stopping and learning rate decay during segmentation training.
use.testing: Used for final evaluation of the segmentation model (314 instances) and the VQA model (56 image-question pairs subset).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as Dice coefficient, Jaccard index, and accuracy.

MEDIQA-MAGIC 2025 / DermaVQA-DAS (Segmen.notes: The model was tested on 314 dermatology images. Additional metrics reported: Jaccard index (mean) 0.5410, Dice (mean of max) 0.7512, Dice (mean of mean) 0.6711, Jaccard (mean of max) 0.6377, Jaccard (mean of mean) 0.5538.

DermaVQA-DAS (Closed VQA).notes: Evaluated on 56 image-question pairs as part of the ImageCLEF VQA-Med 2024 task. The model achieved high scores in categories CQID012 and CQID035, and lower performance in CQID034 and CQID036.

architecture_used.main_model: Two distinct models: 1) Multi-Scale Feature Fusion Network (MSFNet) for segmentation; 2) Multimodal Visual Question Answering (VQA) system combining Vision Transformer (ViT), Local Binary Patterns (LBP), and CLIP.
architecture_used.pretraining: Not explicitly reported for MSFNet (described as 'original MSFNet architecture' reused without modification). For VQA, CLIP and Vision Transformer are implied to be pre-trained models used for feature extraction, but specific pretraining datasets or versions are not detailed.
architecture_used.input_size: 352 x 352 pixels (for segmentation model; images and masks resized to this dimension).

segmentation_model.name: MSFNet
segmentation_model.backbone: Deep CNN with five convolutional blocks (Conv1–Conv5)
modules: Boundary Attention (BA) modules on intermediate layers (Conv2, Conv3)
Parallel Partial Decoder (PPD) processing deep features from Conv4 and Conv5
Reverse Attention (RA) modules for iterative refinement

vqa_model.feature_fusion: Outer product-like matching to capture cross-modal associations
vqa_model.embedding_dimensions: 512-dimensional text embedding (CLIP) + 512-dimensional visual embedding = 1024-dimensional fused feature vector

segmentation_model.lr_decay: Factor of 0.1 if validation loss did not improve for 10 consecutive epochs
segmentation_model.loss_function: Hybrid loss: L_total = 1 * L_WBCE + 1 * L_IoU (Weighted Binary Cross-Entropy + Intersection-over-Union Loss)

vqa_model.hardware_optimization: Mixed precision training on CUDA-enabled GPUs

classification_or_output_head.segmentation_model: Sigmoid activation for binary segmentation mask

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Multimodal VQA model (combining Vision Transformer (ViT), Local Binary Patterns (LBP), CLIP, and BERT-based text encoder)
preprocessing_techniques: Resizing input images and ground truth masks to 352 x 352 pixels for segmentation model
Upsampling predicted segmentation masks to original image resolution using bilinear interpolation during inference
Data augmentation: flipping, rotation, and contrast adjustment during training
Dual-path feature extraction for VQA: Vision Transformer (ViT) and Local Binary Patterns (LBP)
Text encoding using BERT-based model for clinical questions
Feature fusion via outer product-like matching for cross-modal associations in VQA
Concatenation of 512-dimensional text embedding (CLIP) and 512-dimensional visual embedding to form 1024-dimensional fused feature vector

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied during training to enhance generalization and prevent overfitting.
Applied during training to enhance generalization and prevent overfitting.
Applied during training to enhance generalization and prevent overfitting.

conclusions: The MSFNet-based segmentation framework demonstrated strong performance on the MEDIQA-MAGIC 2025 dataset, achieving a mean Dice coefficient of 0.7021 and a mean Jaccard index of 0.5410 across 314 skin lesion instances.
The hybrid loss formulation, combining Weighted Binary Cross-Entropy and IoU losses, was effective in handling class imbalance and enhancing boundary delineation.
The segmentation model showed robustness and generalization capability in binary lesion segmentation.
The closed-ended Visual Question Answering (VQA) model attained an overall accuracy of 56.98% on a test set of 56 image-question pairs.
The VQA model performed notably well in specific question categories, specifically CQID012 (74.80%) and CQID035 (74.00%).
Lower performance in certain VQA categories (e.g., CQID034 and CQID036) reveals areas for improvement in the model's ability to interpret visual inputs for those specific domains.

The integration of segmentation and VQA in a single pipeline highlights potential for real-world applications, including clinical decision support, assistive tools, and automated medical interpretation.

Future work aims to extend the framework to multi-class segmentation, integrate advanced attention modules, explore alternative loss strategies, and optimize the model for real-time clinical or mobile deployment.

Future VQA improvements will focus on expanding training data diversity, incorporating larger-scale multimodal datasets, and experimenting with transformer-based and attention-driven fusion architectures to boost generalization and accuracy.
important_remarks: The segmentation model (MSFNet) was reused without structural modification from a prior work [5], limiting the novelty of the architectural contribution.
The study lacks external validation; the segmentation model was evaluated on only 314 images from a single dataset (MEDIQA-MAGIC 2025/DermaVQA-DAS), raising concerns about generalizability.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed; results are reported solely as descriptive metrics.
The VQA task was evaluated on a very small test set of only 56 image-question pairs, which may lead to high variance in accuracy estimates.

The VQA model achieved a relatively low overall accuracy of 56.98%, with significant performance disparity across question categories (ranging from 35.00% to 74.80%), indicating instability in specific clinical contexts.
The paper claims support for 'early melanoma detection' in the abstract, but the segmentation task is binary (lesion vs. background) and does not classify specific histological types like melanoma.
The authors disclosed the use of generative AI (ChatGPT) for grammar checking and paraphrasing, though they assert full responsibility for the content.
The VQA model relies on pre-trained components (CLIP, ViT) without detailing specific versions or pre-training datasets, which may affect reproducibility.
The segmentation training used a hybrid loss function with empirically set coefficients (alpha=1, beta=1) without ablation studies to justify these hyperparameters.
